description:
Your team’s container-based CI/CD workers all inherit their environment from the normal, unprivileged “user” account.  
    At present they run in UTC and with the default POSIX locale, but a new project requirement mandates:

    • All pipeline shells must run in the Europe/Berlin time zone (CET/CEST).  
    • The default locale for every shell launched by the CI worker must be “en_US.UTF-8”.

    You have to bring the current machine into compliance **without using root privileges** and in a way that the CI runner simply “picks up” the settings by reading the user’s dot-files.

    Required final state (this is what the automated tests will check):

    1. Directory structure  
       a)  /home/user/ci/ exists.  
       b)  /home/user/ci/config/ exists.  
       c)  /home/user/ci/logs/   exists.

    2. Configuration script  
       • A file named /home/user/ci/config/timezone_locale.sh exists.  
       • Its contents – in **exact order** – must be:  
         Line 1:  export TZ="Europe/Berlin"  
         Line 2:  export LANG="en_US.UTF-8"  
         Line 3:  export LC_ALL="en_US.UTF-8"  
         (No extra spaces, no trailing comments, and each line terminated with a single LF.)

    3. Login shell integration  
       • The very last line of /home/user/.bashrc must be:  
         source /home/user/ci/config/timezone_locale.sh  
         (again: a single LF line ending and nothing after it).  

       Note: Do **not** add the source line anywhere else; it must be the last line.

    4. Verification logs  
       After you have loaded the new environment in your current shell, create two log files:  

       a)  /home/user/ci/logs/time_check.log  
           It must contain exactly one line with the result of  
           date --date='2024-01-01 12:00:00 UTC' +'%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S %Z'  
           when TZ is set to Europe/Berlin.  
           For Berlin this timestamp converts to:  
           2024-01-01 13:00:00 CET  

           The log file must therefore hold precisely:  
           2024-01-01 13:00:00 CET  
           (single LF and nothing else).  

       b)  /home/user/ci/logs/locale_check.log  
           It must contain two lines, **in this order**, reflecting the active variables:  
           LANG=en_US.UTF-8  
           LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8  
           (each followed by a single LF).

    5. Command budget  
       The whole job can be accomplished in roughly 5–10 reasonable shell commands; stay within that ballpark.

    Summary: create the directory tree, write the config script, append the single sourcing line to ~/.bashrc, source it in your current shell, produce the two verification logs with the exact contents given above.  No sudo, no system-wide locale generation – everything happens within the user’s homedir.

    When you are done, you can cat the two log files to the console; their exact contents will be used by the automated checker.
truth:
Expected artifacts after successful completion:

    1.  File: /home/user/ci/config/timezone_locale.sh
        Content (3 lines, LF endings):
        --------------------------------------------------
        export TZ="Europe/Berlin"
        export LANG="en_US.UTF-8"
        export LC_ALL="en_US.UTF-8"
        --------------------------------------------------

    2.  File: /home/user/.bashrc
        • The original content is unknown to the task, but the **very last line** must be:
          source /home/user/ci/config/timezone_locale.sh\n

    3.  File: /home/user/ci/logs/time_check.log
        Content (1 line, LF ending):
        --------------------------------------------------
        2024-01-01 13:00:00 CET
        --------------------------------------------------

    4.  File: /home/user/ci/logs/locale_check.log
        Content (2 lines, LF endings):
        --------------------------------------------------
        LANG=en_US.UTF-8
        LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8
        --------------------------------------------------

    Directory permissions: all new directories/files can use default umask-derived permissions (0700 for dirs, 0600 for files is OK).

    The grading script will:
      • check that the three directories exist,
      • verify the exact byte-for-byte contents of the three files listed above.
